does: This paper investigates whether sparse autoencoders (SAEs) can effectively capture concept manifolds—low-dimensional geometric structures where concepts vary continuously rather than along isolated linear directions. The authors develop a theoretical framework characterizing two mechanisms by which SAEs could capture manifolds: global capture through a compact set of atoms whose span contains the entire manifold, or local capture through distributed features that each tile specific manifold regions. They demonstrate empirically that standard SAE architectures recover continuous structures suboptimally, mixing global and local solutions in a degraded regime termed "dilution," which explains the rarity of visible manifold structure in practice despite SAEs' widespread use for interpretability.
connects: This work extends research on neural network interpretability and mechanistic understanding by questioning a foundational assumption underlying SAE-based interpretation: that concepts map to independent linear directions. It responds to accumulating evidence that concepts exhibit manifold structure, building on neuroscience and machine learning findings that biological and artificial neural networks encode information along geometric manifolds. The paper connects to broader interpretability literature examining how neural networks organize representations, as well as prior work on SAEs as tools for decomposing distributed representations. It also engages with geometric deep learning and the study of latent space geometry, applying these insights to understand feature learning in modern language models and vision systems.
enables: This framework establishes foundations for redesigning SAE architectures optimized for manifold capture, suggesting future work could develop modifications addressing the dilution problem through better atoms allocation, nonlinear reconstruction pathways, or alternative loss functions. The theoretical characterization enables more principled approaches to using SAEs for interpretability when concepts have continuous structure, potentially improving our ability to understand and audit neural network behavior. Furthermore, the manifold capture perspective could inspire new probing techniques and evaluation metrics for mechanistic interpretability work, and may inform how we design sparse representations for specific domains where geometric structure is known to be important, such as language embeddings or image representations.
